title: ADK-401: Dynamic Interfaces with A2UI
description: Create interactive user interfaces generated dynamically by agents. Learn the A2UI schema format to build cards, forms, tables, and secure callbacks.

highlights.title: Declarative Layouts
highlights.description: Design rich structural visual component blocks (forms, cards, tables) in agent JSON payloads.

highlights.title: Input Capturing
highlights.description: Implement secure data submission handlers to collect user responses and pass them back.

highlights.title: Sandboxed UI
highlights.description: Enforce strict XSS and code injection checks on all agent-generated interfaces.

blocks.content: Introduction to A2UI Protocol

Text inputs and markdown outputs are insufficient for complex actions like scheduling meetings or reviewing code changes. The Agent-to-User Interface (A2UI) protocol allows agents to emit structured JSON UI components that are rendered as clean, native visual elements in client browsers.
Core Design Philosophy

A2UI separates interface definitions from the rendering engine. The agent outputs UI blocks defined by a strict JSON Schema, and the client frontend platform converts these definitions into styling-consistent React or HTML widgets.
A2UI Container Example Schema
Below is the layout JSON for a basic A2UI block describing a system alert card:
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the primary benefit of the A2UI protocol over standard raw markdown text outputs?

- A) It speeds up the LLM text generation latency.
- B) It allows agents to generate structured, interactive UI blocks like forms, calendars, and tables rendered natively on the client.
- C) It translates agent codes into Go.
Feedback: Option B is correct. A2UI gives the agent the ability to define functional, structured interface layouts that render as native browser widgets.

modules.title: 2. Building Forms and Processing Input Paybacks

blocks.content: Form Components & Input Capture
To capture parameters from the user securely, agents construct forms using the A2UI input widgets. When the user submits, a callback request payload is routed back to the agent session.
A2UI Form Schema Example
This schema defines a user settings form with a text input, selection dropdown, and a submit button:
Understanding Callback Paybacks
When the button is clicked, the UI generates a callback payload containing values from the input fields:
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What format does A2UI use to return user-entered form data to the agent?

- A) As a raw text message containing prompt inputs.
- B) As a structured JSON callback envelope mapping input IDs to values.
- C) As a CSV spreadsheet export.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Data collected from A2UI forms is returned in a structured JSON callback payload with the input values mapped to control IDs.

modules.title: 3. Structuring Cards, Lists, and Data Tables

blocks.content: Cards, Lists, & Data Tables
Rendering structured records (like server lists or database tables) inside markdown text blocks is hard to read. A2UI tables support sorting metadata and visual column formatting.
A2UI Table Definition Schema
Use the following schema format to represent inventory query records dynamically inside the visual panel:

blocks.title: Client rendering
blocks.content: Tables rendered via A2UI automatically include user interface wrappers for local pagination, column resizing, and row selections.
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: How can you specify that a column header in an A2UI data table should be sortable by the end-user?

- A) Set 'sortable': true within the header configuration object.
- B) Include a custom CSS script in the layout configuration.
- C) Prefix the column header label with an asterisk.
Feedback: Option A is correct. Adding 'sortable': true to a header object tells the client-side renderer to add sorting triggers for that column.
Event Security & Verification

Because A2UI elements route events directly back to agents, executing scripts within these components presents security risks. Enforcing sandboxed execution policies prevents malicious inputs from executing commands.
Threat Vector: Callback Injection
An attacker could manipulate form submission values to send shell escape sequences, hoping the agent runs them directly in tools. Security policies must follow three rules:

items: Input Type Enforcement: Validate that numerical fields contain only integers/decimals prior to dispatching tools.
CSRF Token Protection: Attach a session token header to all UI action events to verify the click originated from an active workspace interface.
Sanitization Filters: Strip potential script elements and command pipeline operators (e.g. ;, &&, |) from text inputs.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: Which security mechanism validates that an A2UI submission event originated from the active user's session and not a spoofed network request?

- A) Python type hints
- B) Session CSRF tokens in event headers
- C) Markdown validation filters
Feedback: Option B is correct. Event routing uses CSRF tokens to verify session authenticity.

modules.title: 5. Lab - Creating an Agent-Driven Approval Dashboard

blocks.content: Lab: Deploying an Approval Interface
In this lab, you will write an agent that reviews expense report submissions and generates an interactive A2UI card with 'Approve' and 'Reject' buttons for manager validation.
Step 1: Write the Dashboard Script
Create `src/agents/approval_manager.py` to generate the A2UI approval card layout payload:
Step 2: Start the agent server
Launch your local agent host server to listen for events:
Step 3: Test action callbacks
Interact with the generated visual interface card. Click **Approve**. Review system console log to verify that the callback event routing triggers the correct backend handler function:

blocks.code: [adk-server] Received event: a2ui_submit -> action_id: approve_expense. Processing payment ledger update...
